claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is your child?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please let me know their names and ages, and we can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually asking about three of my children. 

I've got Alex who's 5, Blake who's 17, and Charlie who's 19. Can we check if I'm eligible for all of them?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hi! You're very welcome – I'm happy to help you check eligibility for all three of your children. Let's go through each one.

Let me start with **Alex (age 5)**, since children under 16 have the most straightforward path.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Alex is only 5 years old, this work requirement doesn't apply to young children. Alex clearly isn't working, so we can proceed.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: At age 5, Alex wouldn't be receiving benefits in their own right, so we can continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: A 5-year-old won't be in any apprenticeship, so we can continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask you some questions that will apply to **all three children** since these are about you as the claimant.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **ANY** of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and family
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK – I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you! Living in the UK means you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are you responsible for the children?**

You qualify if **ANY** of the following apply:

1. **You live with the children**
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** (at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you for Alex, Blake, and Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they all live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That confirms you're responsible for all three children. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of your children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie)?**

For example, this could be another parent, a grandparent, or someone else. 

- **No one else is claiming** for any of them
- **Someone else was claiming**, but we've agreed I should claim instead
- **There's a dispute** about who should claim

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for any of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things straightforward. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are any of your children currently in any of these situations?**

- **In hospital**
- **In local authority care**
- **Being fostered**
- **Living at home normally** (none of the above)

I assume since they all live at home with you, they're all living normally – is that correct for Alex, Blake, and Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right – they're all living at home normally with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**One last question that applies to all your children:**

Is the local council **NOT** paying towards any of your children's accommodation or maintenance?

(In other words, you're supporting them without financial assistance from the council for their care)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct – the council isn't paying anything towards their care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That completes the assessment for **Alex (age 5)** – Alex is eligible!

Now let me go back and check **Blake (age 17)** and **Charlie (age 19)**, since children aged 16-19 have some additional requirements around education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now for **Blake (17)** and **Charlie (19)**:

**Are Blake and Charlie currently in education or training?**

For each of them, please let me know if they are:

1. **In education or approved training** (e.g., school, college, sixth form, or an approved training programme)
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks)
3. **Neither** – not in education and haven't been recently

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Blake left school and isn't in any education or training now. 

Charlie is different though – he's studying for his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that – it's helpful to know their different situations.

Let me check **Blake (17)** first, since Blake has recently left school:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Blake (17)**, there may be a **20-week extension period** available. This applies if Blake:
- Left approved education/training **within the last 20 weeks**, AND
- Has registered with a qualifying service (such as a government careers service, Connexions, or armed services)

The extension ends at 20 weeks or when Blake turns 18, whichever comes first.

**Two questions about Blake:**

1. **When did Blake leave school?** (Was it within the last 20 weeks?)
2. **Has Blake registered with any careers service** (like a government careers service or Connexions)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake left school a while ago now – it's been more than 20 weeks. And no, Blake hasn't registered with any careers service or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for letting me know. Unfortunately, that means Blake doesn't qualify for the 20-week extension period.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry, but **Blake is not currently eligible** for Child Benefit because:
- Blake is over 16 and not in approved education or training
- The 20-week extension period has expired
- Blake hasn't registered with a qualifying careers service

However, **if Blake returns to approved education or training** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study), you could potentially claim again.

---

Now let me check **Charlie (19)**, who is studying A-levels at sixth form college:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news – **A-levels are an approved qualification** for Child Benefit purposes! I just need to confirm a couple of things about Charlie's studies:

1. **Is Charlie studying at least 12 hours per week of supervised study?** (Full-time sixth form would typically meet this)

2. **Is Charlie married or in a civil partnership?** (If so, they would need to be financially dependent on you to qualify)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie is studying full-time at sixth form, so definitely more than 12 hours a week. And no, Charlie isn't married.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great news for Charlie's eligibility. A-levels are an approved qualification and Charlie meets the study requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **Does Charlie work less than 24 hours per week** (or not have a paid job at all)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't work at all – just focuses on the studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **Is Charlie NOT receiving benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right – Charlie doesn't get any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Charlie is studying A-levels at sixth form (not in an apprenticeship), we can continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: We've already confirmed that you live in the UK, are responsible for your children, no one else is claiming, they live at home normally, and the council isn't paying towards their care – so Charlie meets all those requirements too.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I've now completed the assessment for all three children. Let me provide you with the final summary:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 📋 Child Benefit Eligibility Summary

### ✅ Alex (age 5) – **ELIGIBLE**
As a child under 16 living at home with you, Alex automatically qualifies for Child Benefit.

### ❌ Blake (age 17) – **NOT ELIGIBLE**
Blake is over 16, no longer in education or training, and the 20-week extension period has passed. To become eligible again, Blake would need to either:
- Return to approved education or training (at least 12 hours/week), or
- Start an approved apprenticeship (e.g., Foundation Apprenticeship in Wales)

### ✅ Charlie (age 19) – **ELIGIBLE**
Charlie qualifies because they're studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college, which is an approved non-advanced qualification. Eligibility will continue until Charlie finishes or leaves this course, or turns 20 (whichever comes first).

---

## 📝 Next Steps

To claim Child Benefit for **Alex** and **Charlie**, you can:

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and the children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for both children

### ⚠️ Important Note
If you or your partner earns over **£60,000** per year, you may be affected by the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**. You can still claim, but some or all of the benefit may need to be repaid through tax. You can choose to receive the payments or just get National Insurance credits.

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the claims process?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) is eligible. As a child under 16 living at home with you, Alex automatically qualifies. You meet the residency requirements (living in the UK), you're responsible for Alex (living together), no one else is claiming, and the council isn't paying towards Alex's care.

Blake (age 17) is NOT eligible. Blake is over 16, has left school more than 20 weeks ago, is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a qualifying careers service (government careers service, Connexions, or armed services) to qualify for the 20-week extension period.

Charlie (age 19) is eligible. Charlie is studying A-levels (an approved, non-advanced qualification) full-time at sixth form college (more than 12 hours per week of supervised study). Charlie is not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not married, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. You meet all the claimant requirements as well.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for 2 out of your 3 children. Alex qualifies automatically as a child under 16. Charlie qualifies as a 16-19 year old in approved full-time education (A-levels). Blake does not currently qualify because Blake is 17, not in education or training, and the 20-week extension period after leaving school has expired without registering with a qualifying careers service.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

response.reason: 20-week extension not applicable (child not registered with qualifying service, extension period expired, or child turned 18)

The extension ends after 20 weeks or when the child turns 18, whichever is sooner. If extension has expired and child is not in approved education/training, Child Benefit stops.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 5) is eligible. As a child under 16 living at home with you, Alex automatically qualifies. You meet the residency requirements (living in the UK), you're responsible for Alex (living together), no one else is claiming, and the council isn't paying towards Alex's care.

Blake (age 17) is NOT eligible. Blake is over 16, has left school more than 20 weeks ago, is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a qualifying careers service (government careers service, Connexions, or armed services) to qualify for the 20-week extension period.

Charlie (age 19) is eligible. Charlie is studying A-levels (an approved, non-advanced qualification) full-time at sixth form college (more than 12 hours per week of supervised study). Charlie is not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not married, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. You meet all the claimant requirements as well.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for 2 out of your 3 children. Alex qualifies automatically as a child under 16. Charlie qualifies as a 16-19 year old in approved full-time education (A-levels). Blake does not currently qualify because Blake is 17, not in education or training, and the 20-week extension period after leaving school has expired without registering with a qualifying careers service.
